title: Shook swarm method for bees
subtitle: Moving the colony onto fresh comb to break a disease cycle.
excerpt: The shook swarm moves the entire adult bee population onto clean foundation, leaving all old comb behind. It interrupts the reproductive cycle of Varroa, clears contaminated stores, and is the recommended intervention when a colony shows signs of European foulbrood or has heavy Varroa burden.
sourceNotes: NBU Advisory leaflet 10, European Foulbrood and the Shook Swarm method. APHA Bee Health Programme. NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), disease control.

content.text: The shook swarm is the standard UK fix for European foulbrood and for breaking a heavy Varroa cycle. It moves all the adult bees onto clean foundation and destroys all old comb. That breaks the disease cycle. It also cuts the mite count, because Varroa breeds in the sealed brood that is now gone. Do it in spring, once the colony is large enough to draw fresh comb quickly. Usually that is when the trees are coming into blossom.

attrs.body: European foulbrood confirmed by a laboratory test is a notifiable disease under the Bee Diseases and Pests Control Order. Contact the APHA bee inspector before doing a shook swarm if you suspect EFB rather than treating presumptively. The inspector may prescribe oxytetracycline alongside the shook swarm. Do not move frames off the holding.
attrs.title: EFB is notifiable

content.text: Prepare a clean hive body with a full set of new frames fitted with foundation. Place it on the existing hive stand. Have a rapid feeder ready with sugar syrup: 1 kg sugar to 1 litre water. The bees will be on bare foundation with no stores after the shook swarm and need immediate feeding to draw comb.

Suit up and light the smoker. Remove the old hive body from the stand and set it to one side. Place the new clean box on the stand. Work through the old box frame by frame, shaking the bees from each frame sharply over the new box. Three or four firm shakes per frame deposits most of the bees; a brush can clear stubborn clusters on the last few frames. Return each old frame to the old hive body as you work.
The queen will be on one of the frames. When you see her, either let her fall into the new box with the bees or place her on the new foundation directly. Do not leave her in the old box.

If the shook swarm was done for EFB or another notifiable disease, bag all old frames in double-bin bags, seal them, and burn. Do not render the wax or give the frames to another beekeeper. If the shook swarm was for Varroa management only (not disease), old frames can be scraped and the wax rendered in a solar wax melter or double-boiler for candle use, keeping the wax off any live colony.

Apply the rapid feeder immediately. In the absence of brood, the bees are entirely focused on drawing the new comb and need the energy. Check after four days: fresh comb should be drawn on at least five frames. At day 10, confirm the queen is laying. A shook swarm done in April on a strong colony should have several frames of sealed brood by day 21.
